summary: Graham argues that taste is real and improvable, not mere personal preference, and that designers in any field need it to make good things. He outlines shared principles of good design—simplicity, timelessness, solving the right problem, suggestiveness, humor, difficulty, ease, symmetry, resemblance to nature, redesign, and informed copying—showing how they converge across disciplines.
key_points: Taste is objective enough to improve; if you can get better at design, your old tastes were worse.
Good design consistently emphasizes simplicity, timelessness, and solving the right problem.
Great work comes from exacting taste plus the skill and effort to satisfy it.
money_quotes: When you're forced to be simple, you're forced to face the real problem.
If you can imagine someone surpassing you, you should do it yourself.
Good design may not have to be funny, but it's hard to imagine something that could be called humorless also being good design.
The recipe for great work is: very exacting taste, plus the ability to gratify it.
The only style worth having is the one you can't help.
key_insight: Great design across fields comes from cultivated, exacting taste applied to simple, timeless solutions to the right problems.
